front: Architect: first woman to win the Pritzker Prize (2004); Iraqi-born British architect who died in 2016; known as the 'queen of the curve'
Architect: studied mathematics before studying architecture in London; studied under Rem Koolhaas and Elia Zenghelis at the Architectural Association
Architect: firm partner Patrik Schumacher coined the term 'parametricism'; began as a painter — created The Peak Project depicting a Kowloon project
Building: opera house on the Pearl River in Guangzhou, China — design inspired by two rocks being smoothed/eroded by the river ('twin boulders'); mostly glass
Building: Guangzhou opera house uses reinforced gypsum; also called a 'double pebble' design
Building: 2012 Summer Olympics facility — wave-shaped / undulating roof; concrete ceiling slopes toward the pool between two walls of windows
Building: Olympic swimming venue whose spectator wings were added for the Games and later removed
Building: Italy's National Museum of 21st Century Arts in Rome — built on a former military barracks; features floating/weaving stairs, flowing concrete beams, and louvered skylights
Building: Rome museum with a sharply angular concrete facade overhang inspired by Constructivist art; described as moving away from 'the museum as an object' toward 'a field of buildings'
Building: 2022 FIFA World Cup stadium in Qatar — design inspired by the curvy shape of a dhow fishing boat
work: Al-Wakrah / Al-Janoub Stadium (2022 FIFA World Cup, Qatar)

front: Building: Qatar World Cup stadium — architect sued the New York Review of Books for defamation after being criticized for dismissing concerns over dying migrant construction workers
work: Al-Wakrah / Al-Janoub Stadium (2022 FIFA World Cup, Qatar)

front: Building: won the commission for Japan's 2020 Olympic stadium; design scrapped — Arata Isozaki called it 'a turtle waiting for Japan to sink so that it can swim away'; critics compared it to a bicycle helmet

Building: cultural complex in Seoul — flat, fluid body with 45,000 uniquely curved gray panels; designed using the concept of a 'Metonymic Landscape'; partner Patrik Schumacher coined 'parametricism' partly to describe it
Building: science museum in Wolfsburg, Germany — rests on huge concrete stilts, allowing visitors and vehicles to pass underneath
Building: her first constructed building — in-house fire station for the Vitra campus in Weil am Rhein, Germany, designed after a factory fire
Building: neo-futurist cultural center in Baku, Azerbaijan — named for the country's former president; featured on the 200 manat banknote
Building: Bergisel Ski Jump tower and Hungerburg Funicular stations in Innsbruck — funicular stations are glacier-inspired, serving Nordkette Mountain
back: Bergisel Ski Jump & Hungerburg Funicular, Innsbruck (Zaha Hadid)

front: Building: airport near Beijing nicknamed 'The Starfish'; claimed to be the world's largest airport at opening; completed posthumously by Zaha Hadid Architects
Building: otherwise cube-shaped office tower in Dubai distinguished by a curving, amorphous opening passing through it; completed posthumously
Building: CityLife redevelopment in Milan — Hadid designed 'The Curved One' alongside Libeskind's 'Twisted One' and Isozaki's 'Straight One'
Building: Rosenthal Center / Contemporary Arts Center in Cincinnati — her first American building; the museum involved in the 1990 Robert Mapplethorpe obscenity trial moved here in 2003
Building: BMW Central Building in Leipzig — designed as the 'nerve center' of the factory complex, bringing blue-collar and white-collar employees together
Architect: graduation project 'Malevich's Tektonik' — a Suprematist hotel on Hungerford Bridge; designed the first Serpentine Gallery annual summer pavilion (2000), initiating the yearly tradition
Architect: designed Aqua Table — single piece of polyurethane with three wave-like 'undulations'; also designed 3D-printed shoes for Rem Koolhaas's company United Nude
Architect: Broad Art Museum at Michigan State University — exterior of perpendicular and parallel steel rods above sheets of glass
